name: He'eia Fishpond
tagline: An 800-year-old Hawaiian fishpond being actively restored — a living classroom where ancient aquaculture meets modern sustainability.
price: Free to view; tours by reservation
parking: Parking at He'eia State Park across the road
hours: Grounds visible daily; guided tours and volunteer days scheduled through Paepae o He'eia
bestTime: Join a volunteer restoration day — hands-on experience rebuilding the rock wall while learning from cultural practitioners
talkStory: This 800-year-old, 88-acre loko i'a is one of the last intact ancient fishponds on Oahu, managed by Paepae o He'eia, a Native Hawaiian nonprofit. Ancient Hawaiians engineered makaha (sluice gates) to let small fish in from Kaneohe Bay but trap them once they grew — sustainable farming genius. The kuapa (rock wall) stretches nearly a mile and was built by hand from basalt and coral. Sign up for a volunteer day to help restore the wall while learning from cultural practitioners — one of the most meaningful experiences on Oahu.
